title: Google I/O 2025 – o2wkYzu5u50
text:
[MUSIC PLAYING] PAUL KINLAN: Oh,
isn't it a great time to be a web developer, eh? How many times does
HTML in the key? Oh, it's brilliant,
I was amazed. Anyway, I'm Paul Kinlan. I lead the Developer
Relations team in Chrome. But before we dive in, I've got
a little demo to show you today. It's not the best looking demo. It was made by
me, but I think it tries to demonstrate
everything that we're working on inside
Chrome, at least, anyway. So I'm learning Welsh. I live in North Wales. My daughter speaks Welsh in
school, Japanese at home. Learning languages is
unbelievably hard for me. And I have a Welsh
teacher, and the way that she teaches me is she gives
me images and then she asks me, what is in the image
in Welsh, basically. And I have to describe it. As a learning method, it
works surprisingly well. So I built a tool to help me. Can we just switch
over to the demo? So this is the demo that I
built. Not a vibe code in sight. It was all handcrafted
by me, as you can tell. So this is the new
select element. We can style
everything inside this. I think it's pretty neat. We have this proficiency
mode where you can select the level, a language level. It's got Brit mode because Brits
are really good at learning languages, right? We have capture. So what I'm going to do is
I'm going to capture an image. And what we're
going to do is we're going to send it off on device
to our multimodal model, prompt model, and
it's going to generate a whole list of questions
for us to practice on. So here we go. There we go. It's a nice photo, I think. So in the background, it's going
through the multimodal model. We have this little hover
thing here so it's already got the description. No JavaScript in sight. It's just a bit of HTML, popover
and the interest invoker. Here we go. We have the questions. And I can delete some of these
using view transitions to get rid of elements from the Dom. We've never been able
to animate them before-- unbelievably brilliant. And then if we press Play,
I've sent every single question
speaker: unknown

title: Google I/O 2025 – o2wkYzu5u50
text:
And I can delete some of these
using view transitions to get rid of elements from the Dom. We've never been able
to animate them before-- unbelievably brilliant. And then if we press Play,
I've sent every single question through our new
translation API that's on device, no data leaving. And this generated
the list of questions which are now in
our carousel that we showed a little bit earlier. And then I think this is
what color is my shirt? Green. Here we go. It's gone off and checked. It says, I got it wrong because
I didn't answer in French. But this is the power of
what we've got right now is that with just a
small bit of JavaScript and everything in
HTML and CSS, we can build beautiful applications. Well, we can build better
ones than that look like this. Can we just switch
back now, please, to the main presentation? Cool. So what I'm going
to talk about today is how we're, hopefully, making
the ecosystem more powerful, more capable and easier to use. And you'll be able
to build applications that look and feel way better
than the one I just built now. But you'll be able to do it
just with a small piece of HTML, JavaScript, and CSS. So I live with the web. I grew up around it. This is me in 1998 when monitors
were tiny but unbelievably heavy. And this is, literally, the
best photo that we've got. It's incredibly grainy. But I had hair. I can't believe it. It was incredible. My mom loves it still. Anyway, I've been
here long enough, and working in the
web for long enough, to see huge amount of
different transitions. The dial up to broadband
transition, desktop to mobile. Maybe there's an
AI-enhanced transition that's happening right now. But fundamentally,
the web has just changed so much in
the last 30 years, and it's not slowing down. In fact, the web is accelerating
in capability, in performance and, I think, also,
in opportunity. And it's at the center of every
major technological shift. The browser is now
an operating system. It works for
everyone, everywhere, across mobile, desktop,
tablet, on your server,
speaker: unknown

title: Google I/O 2025 – o2wkYzu5u50
text:
and, I think, also,
in opportunity. And it's at the center of every
major technological shift. The browser is now
an operating system. It works for
everyone, everywhere, across mobile, desktop,
tablet, on your server, even in your car. It's amazing. But before we dive in, I just
want to acknowledge something about this presentation today. Chrome has changed so
much in the last year with hundreds of new
features just chipped, amazing capabilities,
CSS, JavaScript, and HTML. We've only got about
30 minutes, so I'm not going to be able
to cover everything that we're going
to-- well, everything that's coming through Chrome. I've just got a
couple of major themes that I want to try
and talk about today. But there is a
major tension that I do want to talk about first. The web means so much to
me because it just gives us the ability to do what
no other platform can do. You can just write once
and reach everyone. And the web's
superpower is that it's a medium that is accessible
to all in any browser just by clicking on the link. And I think that's an
incredibly powerful. And, yes, in Chrome
we build features to demonstrate the
future of the web that we think is
needed on the platform. But no matter what
that Chrome does, the web only advances when
all browsers advance together. And also, at the same time, the
common perception of the web is, it can't do this
and it can't do that. It's just this pervasive
story everywhere. But the web is more capable,
and it is more versatile than we give it credit for. And the question is like, what
is causing that perception gap? And I think it's two things. It's clarity and compatibility. There are two big major
areas where developers just don't have enough confidence. And it comes from
a lot of issues. But browsers have either
prioritized different web standards. We all implement
different things, and sometimes we implement
them differently, as well. And this makes it very
difficult for developers to understand what features
work well across all browsers.
speaker: unknown

title: Google I/O 2025 – o2wkYzu5u50
text:
standards. We all implement
different things, and sometimes we implement
them differently, as well. And this makes it very
difficult for developers to understand what features
work well across all browsers. And so we are working
with other browsers to address this in two ways. The first you might know, is
called the Interop Project. And the Interop addresses
compatibility issues by bringing browser
vendors together to identify focus areas
that you, the developers, have said are important
for you to fix. In 2025, we hope things
like anchor positioning, view transitions,
the navigation API, we hope that these
are all addressed so that they're available across
the entirety of the platform. And the second is
the Baseline project, which you heard a little
bit more about today. Now, the goal of
Baseline is it gives you clarity on what feature
areas, things like CSS grid, are supported
across all browsers, and it breaks it down
by feature availability into three different
categories-- limited availability, newly
available, and widely available. So limited availability is
when the feature is only in one or two
browsers, and it's only really usable with polyfills
or other different mitigations. You're very unlikely to use
it for a production site, but they're interesting
APIs at that time. But when a feature
becomes interoperable, it's the moment
when it's available across all major
browser engines, we consider it newly available. Now, it still might
not be ready for you to use in your production sites
based on your browser support, but it's at least something
that you can start to plan for. And then the third
stage, after 30 months, we consider it widely available. And this is the point when we
think developers can confidently use the feature on the platform. Now, I'm incredibly
excited by Baseline. For the first time that
we can see as the web, as experienced by the
people that use it, is improving faster
than ever before. Since 2020, the web has expanded
with 196 different features that have become widely available.
speaker: unknown

title: Google I/O 2025 – o2wkYzu5u50
text:
For the first time that
we can see as the web, as experienced by the
people that use it, is improving faster
than ever before. Since 2020, the web has expanded
with 196 different features that have become widely available. I mean, I actually tell a lie. I checked last night
and it's 197 features. They wouldn't let me
update the slides. The slides had to be locked. But it's features like
streams, broadcast channels that isn't where
pseudo selectors, and the dialogue element. These are all things that
are available for you to use in your experiences. And we expect at least 150 more
features to become available over the next 30 months,
things like popover, :has(), container queries,
HTTP/3, new set methods. They're all becoming widely
available in the coming months, and I just don't think we've
seen the level of progress for such a long time. And I think it's amazing
to see all the progress that all the browser vendors
have achieved with this project. And we're also making it
easier for you, as developers, to keep up with the
latest improvements. The web platform
dashboard at webstatus.dev tells you exactly what features
are Baseline, newly available, and widely available. And the feature dashboard
focuses on feature area so that you don't
have to look up individual statuses
of each feature. And the great thing
is, the data that drives Baseline is the data
that drives the web platform dashboard. And this data is also available
for you, as developers, to include in your tooling. Now, you might have already seen
the data being used on web.dev, and MDN. MDN integrated
Baseline support across over 70% of all their pages. But there's lots of other
ways that Baseline data can help you as a developer. So RUMvision, they wanted
to help their customers figure out which Baseline
features were available and supported by
their customers users, and they combined their Baseline
data with their real user monitoring data, and discovered
that their customers were serving polyfills for about
97% of their customers
speaker: unknown

title: Google I/O 2025 – o2wkYzu5u50
text:
and supported by
their customers users, and they combined their Baseline
data with their real user monitoring data, and discovered
that their customers were serving polyfills for about
97% of their customers when they didn't need to. And by looking at your
site's actual usage data, you can then target a
specific baseline year and make more informed
decisions about which features that you can use in production. But to put the Baseline
target into practice, you can configure your linter,
as we saw in the keynote. And it enables you to
warn your developers and yourself when your features
you're using in your code go beyond your Baseline target. And so if you use ESLint,
there are two plugins that support Baseline. First up is the
CSS lint by ESLint with the Baseline rule
configured by default. And the second is the community
plugin HTML ESLint that has also added its own Baseline
support by default. And if you use
Stylelint, there's an officially supported
Baseline plugin for you, too. Now, obviously, we spend
so much time in our ideas, and what we really want to
know when we're coding is is this feature
something I can use? Is it ready or not? Well, wouldn't it be great
if the Baseline information that you need was easy to
access, easy to understand, and right in your environment? Well, now you can. So Baseline is now available
in Visual Studio code, and soon, in all downstream
visual VS Code-based IDEs, including Firebase Studio. And as we heard
before, we've also partnered with
JetBrains, who will be adding Baseline support into
their WebStorm IDE in the coming months. Now, whenever you
hover over a feature, you'll be able to tell if
the feature you're using is Baseline newly
available, or widely available all in your
IDE, right, as your code. I just think this is amazing. It's so productive. Now, we are really excited about
all the different tools that are adopting Baseline
and Baseline data and integrating it in,
because the more tools that we have that deliver
these Baseline integrations,
speaker: unknown

title: Google I/O 2025 – o2wkYzu5u50
text:
It's so productive. Now, we are really excited about
all the different tools that are adopting Baseline
and Baseline data and integrating it in,
because the more tools that we have that deliver
these Baseline integrations, the easier it will be
for you as developers to build for the best of
the web and have confidence to ship high-quality sites
that use the latest features. We're obviously going to
continue investing in Baseline so that you can use
those technologies without compromising stability
or cross-browser platform compatibility. And Rachel is going to talk
about this a lot more a little bit later in our talk with
what's new in the web. Now, we've covered a lot
of features that have come available to all browsers. I want to spend a lot more
time now talking about some of the exciting things in Chrome
that we are delivering first. So first up, obviously, is AI
and what it means for the web, and how the web excels, I
think, at delivering AI features and products. Now, I think that the
web is a great platform to deliver AI-enhanced
experiences because millions of users can instantly
access them with just a simple click of the link. You can use massive
Cloud models, like Gemini 2.5 Pro that
have advanced capabilities like writing code, or you can
take advantage of client-side AI with models directly
inside the browser. So client-side AI models, I
think they're really exciting. They've got benefits like
zero network latency. They're cost effective. They can have enhanced privacy. And they even work when
the user is offline. And when adapted
to specific tasks, these models can also match
the performance and quality of server side models, but
without the cost overhead of running on the Cloud. So if you're
comfortable managing your own models and
your own distribution, obviously, you can use
tools like MediaPipe and transformer.js because
they use some of Chrome's new core platform primitives. Technologies like
WebAssembly and WebGPU, they give these libraries
direct access to the hardware,
speaker: unknown

title: Google I/O 2025 – o2wkYzu5u50
text:
obviously, you can use
tools like MediaPipe and transformer.js because
they use some of Chrome's new core platform primitives. Technologies like
WebAssembly and WebGPU, they give these libraries
direct access to the hardware, like the GPU and maybe
new things in the future, as well, directly on
the user's device, allowing them to run the
models in the browser as fast as possible. They let you analyze things like
text, images, video, and audio all in real-time. And they do things like text
sentiment analysis, image classification, tracking
objects inside videos, transforming text into
audio, and even doing things like image-to-image
transformations-- again, all directly
inside the browser. Now, over the last
couple of years, we've asked you
what tools and what features that you really need
to get the most out of AI in the browser. We listened, and we've
built a number of APIs for some of the most common
use cases into the browser. Now, these built-in
AI APIs, they're designed to address two
different challenges. First, you shouldn't have
to spend time reasoning about what is the best model
to use for common tasks. You should just
be able to use it. And Chrome's built-in AI
uses highly efficient models that should just work without
any extra effort from your side. And the second is
that your users shouldn't be bogged down
by the sheer size of some of these models. Now, one model can take
a long time to download. And if the user has to download
it for every single site that they visit, they take
up a lot of storage space and a huge amount of bandwidth. Built-in AI models are
managed by the browser, and are available to all sites. So that means that once a
model has been downloaded once by one model, it's
available to all sites using the built-in AI APIs. Now, I love these APIs. You saw some of them in the
demo a little bit earlier. But I want to show
you some of the APIs that we're shipping really soon. So the first one is
the Summarization API. It's available in
Chrome 138, which I think is Canary, as of today.
speaker: unknown

title: Google I/O 2025 – o2wkYzu5u50
text:
You saw some of them in the
demo a little bit earlier. But I want to show
you some of the APIs that we're shipping really soon. So the first one is
the Summarization API. It's available in
Chrome 138, which I think is Canary, as of today. And it enables you to
compile long articles, complex documents, or
even chat conversations into concise summaries. The API can generate
different types of summaries in various types of lengths,
in different types of formats, like from short paragraphs to
bullet points in plain text and markdown, as well. And it just helps users get a
quick overview of long text. The Language Detector
and Translator API is also available in Chrome 138,
let you detect an unknown source language, and then
translate that text into over 39 different
languages on the fly so that you can build
features that adapt to the user's first language. Now, you also might
need an API that's a little bit more
broad, one that can address a wide range of needs. So at last year's I/O we
announced the Prompt API. And after all the feedback
that we've received, it's now available
in Chrome extensions. This API lets you send natural
language requests to Gemini directly inside the browser. So, for example, you
can create an extension to generate instant calendar
events that extract key details, like the time and
date from websites. Or you can extract contact
information from a website so you can then subsequently,
more easily contact a business. Now, developers are already
using this technology to build impactful Chrome
extensions to help their users, and I think this is actually
a pretty cool example. So many businesses still
rely on printed documents for day-to-day operations, and
digging through physical docs to find what you're looking for. It's slow manual work. And so our friends
at Adobe recently experimented with using
the new prompt API and some multimodal support
in their Acrobat extension, and they can use it to apply
it to analyze a historic stock certificate with faded and
difficult to read text.
speaker: unknown

title: Google I/O 2025 – o2wkYzu5u50
text:
experimented with using
the new prompt API and some multimodal support
in their Acrobat extension, and they can use it to apply
it to analyze a historic stock certificate with faded and
difficult to read text. Now, their users can now
generate text summaries from scanned visuals in Adobe,
all directly within Chrome. And in this case,
a user can then chat directly with the
Acrobat AI Assistant to surface, organize,
and validate key facts. It reduces just a huge
amount of manual work, and enables faster,
more accurate capture of critical
information from even sparse or degraded documents. So the idea of being able
to analyze and understand any type of information inside
the browser, not just text, I think, is a critical
piece of functionality. So we're adding image and
audio input to the prompt API, making it multimodal
for the first time. So you'll be able to describe
the contents of images and produce
summaries and extract information, structured
data from text, images, and audio now. So whether it's
transcribing text from an audio file
in the browser, or extracting handwriting
from an image, you'll be able to build
very advanced applications and run them all
locally on device. Now, the way that you use this
API is actually pretty simple. It's exactly the same way that
you would use the prompt API. You create a session by
calling create and add the new expected inputs option,
and then you call prompt. But then when you
call prompt, you can include image elements,
blobs, image bitmaps, video frames, and
even Canvas objects. And then you just call prompt,
and you'll get the response. Now, I believe that the Prompt
API with multimodal input will open up a brand new
world of experimentation inside the browser. But we still hear that you
want very targeted use cases. And so I want to show
you two more APIs that are going to help with composing
and writing inside the browser. So the first up is
the Proofreader API. It's being developed to help
you compose better prose, compose better-- it rhymes--
speaker: unknown

title: Google I/O 2025 – o2wkYzu5u50
text:
And so I want to show
you two more APIs that are going to help with composing
and writing inside the browser. So the first up is
the Proofreader API. It's being developed to help
you compose better prose, compose better-- it rhymes-- oh, my God-- anyway, so while
using a web application. So this API can label
errors in inputted text, explain it to the
user, and then offer corrections, as well-- again,
all inside the browser. But what if you need a little
bit more inspiration when, say, crafting your
next Blue Sky post? Well, the Writer API can
help you generate content that matches the topic and the
context of a specific task, such as maybe saying how
awesome this talk is-- maybe, I don't know. Anyway, you can also use the
Rewriter API to then adjust the copy to adjust the tone
and length of the content, all to-- you can make it
a little bit more formal as an example. Both of these APIs are now
available as origin trials. And if you want to sign
up, all of these APIs that we described today are
available in Chrome Canary, and we'd love you
to test them out. And if you want to
try them out, you can go to this
URL on the screen. So there are going to be
times when Client-side AI just doesn't work for you. Not all devices can
run models locally because they're not
powerful enough, or they don't have
enough storage. Or even in a lot of
cases, the browser just doesn't support the
APIs that we're working on. So we need this hybrid approach
of preparing Web AI and Cloud together that allow the same
model to run on device and on-- and when capable, will fall
back to the-- when not capable, will fall back to the
Cloud AI, whilst also-- while ensuring a very
consistent and the same response from both sides. So our colleagues
at Firebase have built the hybrid
SDK as an extension to the Firebase Web SDK. And it can detect if
a user's device is capable of running
the model, and then make a decision on
the fly to say, well, I can use the client-side
model, or Gemini on the server. And it's all via one
single implementation.
speaker: unknown

title: Google I/O 2025 – o2wkYzu5u50
text:
And it can detect if
a user's device is capable of running
the model, and then make a decision on
the fly to say, well, I can use the client-side
model, or Gemini on the server. And it's all via one
single implementation. I think that's actually a very
powerful and progressive way of actually building
applications today. So this hybrid API-- this
hybrid SDK is currently available as a special preview,
developer preview for you to be able to test. It's not ready for
production yet, which we hope will be
coming later in the year. So just, obviously,
check out the link. You'll get information
about the developer preview when it will be available
as the final version. Now, while it's great to
see AI capabilities come into the platform,
we, as a team, we're on the Developer
Relations team, we thrive when we help you
deliver high-quality sites that are fast, accessible, and you
can do it quickly and easily. And we think Chrome DevTools
is the premier tool for you to be able to just do that. So Chrome DevTools has
been pioneering features for web development
from the beginning, from debugging layouts to
in-depth performance analysis. So last year, we introduced
Gemini in DevTools with Console Insights
and AI assistance. And we've seen developers
use this like crazy. The ability to interact directly
with Gemini in DevTools, it's just been a game
changer for many developers. But I want to show you also
what we've been working on over the last year and
how DevTools has evolved with powerful new features that
streamline your development journey. [VIDEO PLAYBACK] [MUSIC PLAYING] - Go. Go. [END PLAYBACK] PAUL KINLAN: Brilliant. Well, I am-- oh. Brilliant. [APPLAUSE] I use these features every day. I just love the progress
that we've seen in DevTools. I just want to spend
a couple more moments just actually diving
in a little bit deeper into some of
the AI assistance work and all the features that
we've landed inside DevTools. So we're going to cover some
CSS issues, network issues, and advice on your
site's performance.
speaker: unknown

title: Google I/O 2025 – o2wkYzu5u50
text:
just actually diving
in a little bit deeper into some of
the AI assistance work and all the features that
we've landed inside DevTools. So we're going to cover some
CSS issues, network issues, and advice on your
site's performance. I think the fact that we've
got AI in the browser helping in these areas, it's going to
be a game changer for everyone. So if you're having trouble
with a specific element style, AI assistance for
styling now supports complete end-to-end
debugging of styling issues. So you can select the
Dom element, or Dom node, and then start just a
free-flowing conversation with Gemini about the element. And so if you get stuck, Gemini
will also give you prefilled prompts to suggest what
areas that you can look at. And once you've
identified the problem, you can then prompt
Gemini to fix it, and then even apply the fix directly
back into the source code, all within DevTools,
without ever leaving Chrome. The network panel has also
seen a new Gemini integration, as well. You can ask questions like, why
is this network request taking so long? You can ask it to explain
about security headers, or why even web
requests are failing. And I use this quite frequently. It's just such a good feature
for debugging CDN issues, and things like cause
issues, as well. And the same applies for
the Performance panel. So you can talk to Gemini about
common performance issues, and it will investigate
which particular slow-running functions, or
what particular slow-running functions are doing. And then it will also give
you actionable insights about how to fix those issues
directly inside DevTools, again. And in addition to
the AI enhancements, we've also reimagined
the Performance panel to help you identify and
fix performance issues and just keep your web
applications running fast and smooth. So now, all of your Core
Web Vitals information are in one place, and it now
pulls in real Chrome user experience data next to
your local experience so that you can
ensure you're actually
speaker: unknown

title: Google I/O 2025 – o2wkYzu5u50
text:
fast and smooth. So now, all of your Core
Web Vitals information are in one place, and it now
pulls in real Chrome user experience data next to
your local experience so that you can
ensure you're actually fixing the problems as
experienced by your users, with no recording necessary. And you'll also get advice
on what environment settings to use so that you can match
the majority of your users. And once you've identified the
issue, if you really need to, then you can go and record
a full trace and deep dive in to the issues. Now, the Insights tab
in the Performance panel is now powered by
Lighthouse, giving you guided, actionable insights
about performance issues that are slowing down your site. Here, you'll find LCP
insights for page load issues, INP insights for
responsiveness, and CLS insights for layout stability issues. And if you want to zero in on
specific third party scripts, or concentrate only on the
contributions of slowness from your own APIs, or
your CDN, the Summary tab will now give you a breakdown
of first and third parties that DevTools is able
to find on the page. So for a long time now, DevTools
has been giving you the power to quickly and easily
understand and improve the performance of your site. But what about the
web as a whole? What if everything about
the web was faster? When the web is fast,
more people use it. So I just want to show you
some of how Chrome is pushing web performance forwards. So we introduced the concept
of Core Web Vitals in 2020, and it was our effort
to measure and optimize what we thought were the
essential aspects of a good web page experience through
three key metrics. And in 2024, people saved about
30,000 years of time surfing the web simply because it got
faster in the core metrics that Core Web Vitals tracks. I think that's
incredible, right? What's more, the number
of sites that had good LCP has increased by almost 8%,
and the sites with good INP has increased by over 12%
in the last year alone. I think, yeah, these
are fantastic results,
speaker: unknown

title: Google I/O 2025 – o2wkYzu5u50
text:
I think that's
incredible, right? What's more, the number
of sites that had good LCP has increased by almost 8%,
and the sites with good INP has increased by over 12%
in the last year alone. I think, yeah, these
are fantastic results, and we're excited that
two Core Web Vitals, which are Largest Contentful Paint
and Interaction to Next Paint, will soon be coming
to other browsers as part of the
Interop 2025 Project that we mentioned earlier. Well done, Barry. [APPLAUSE] That means you'll soon
be able to understand how your users'
experience your site, no matter what browser
that you're using. So I just want to spend a
couple of minutes just looking under the hood of
Core Web Vitals and explore some of the
new metrics and APIs that we've worked on. LCP subparts breaks
down the LCP time into four distinct phases
to help you understand where the bottlenecks are, and
they provide crucial insight into LCP image loading
and help you easily identify where to prioritize
improving your site. As well as showing
local LCP subparts, DevTools now also
shows field data from the Chrome user
experience report directly in the same report. And it's also not just us, we're
seeing more performance tools and room providers exposing
this very useful information. Now, we introduced the
Speculation Rules API at last year's I/O as a way of
letting you programmatically tell Chrome which pages
to prefetch and prerender in advance of a user
navigating to them. So the ability to go from fast
loading to instant loading, it can just make a
massive difference in how users
experience your site. And this API has been a
resounding success, with over 8% of page loads now benefiting
from Speculation Rules. And Cloudflare is an internet
security and performance provider serving
over 20% of the web. And last year, Cloudflare
launched Speed Brain, which is a product which uses
speculative loading by default to improve the performance
of sites on Cloudflare. And their testing showed
a 45% improvement in LCP
speaker: unknown

title: Google I/O 2025 – o2wkYzu5u50
text:
And last year, Cloudflare
launched Speed Brain, which is a product which uses
speculative loading by default to improve the performance
of sites on Cloudflare. And their testing showed
a 45% improvement in LCP for successfully prefetch
sites, as compared to non-prefetched sites. And on the INP front,
the scheduler.yield API helps you improve INP by
pausing execution of long tasks, allowing the browser to
attend to other pending tasks, and then resume
the original task. If you ever did Visual Basic,
it's like do events, at least from when I started programming. And we're seeing that over
6% of website page loads now are already using this API. LoAF, which I think
should be called LoAF, but that's an argument
I lost ages ago, brings a Long Animation
Frames API to everyone. Now, a Long Animation Frame is
when a rendered update takes longer than 50 milliseconds. It's the same threshold
as the Long Tasks API. But the key advantage of
looking at this compared to individual tasks is that a
Long Animation Frame can just be the result of
a number of tasks, but it's a point of
visual jank on the page. Additionally, the LoAF API gives
detailed script information, helping you identify the areas
on your site that impact UX, and also, INP. And we're excited to see
that only 10% of websites are using the LoAF API. And last but not
least, I think it's one of the most important
and most impactful new features that
has come to Chrome. Compression Dictionary Transport
is now available in Chrome. And this new standard enables
you to differentially serve static assets so
that you only send what has changed in an
updated version of the file. So by reusing the
previously downloaded file as a dictionary for compression
algorithms, things like Brotli, you can massively reduce the
amount of data transferred, and speed up page loads. So for example, if you've
got a compressed version of your library 1.8.3, and
then you're using version 1.8.2 as your dictionary, the
output could just be a couple
speaker: unknown

title: Google I/O 2025 – o2wkYzu5u50
text:
you can massively reduce the
amount of data transferred, and speed up page loads. So for example, if you've
got a compressed version of your library 1.8.3, and
then you're using version 1.8.2 as your dictionary, the
output could just be a couple of kilobytes, potentially, a
compression ratio up to 98%. And so Google searches use
Compression Dictionary Transport to reduce the size of
its HTML payload by 23%, as compared to the standard
Brotli implementation. It also saw an LCP
improvement by over 2%-- or almost 2%, sorry, and up to
9% in high latency networks. These are huge gains for an
already hyper optimized site. Less optimized sites might
see even more benefit from adopting this technology. So being able to understand
your page's responsiveness and improve loading,
I think this is great. Because when I think
about web performance and I think about making it
easier for people to access experiences on a website. Obviously, the
quicker, the better. But what about when a
user is on the site? So for the past
three years, we've seen an amazing explosion
in the expressiveness of UI capabilities on the web. And these UI features enable you
to build beautiful, responsive, and performant websites
faster and more efficiently than ever before. Take view transitions,
for an example. I think it's just been amazing
to see the progress in just a few years. We started with the same
document view transitions, and then a little
over a year ago, we enabled view transitions between
page navigations, as well. And just recently, as
we saw in the keynote, google.com added
view transitions to power the UI for things
like AI mode in search. It just makes such
an amazing difference to the experience of the web. And a number of frameworks are
also adopting view transitions and making it easier for you
to use this API right out of the box and express custom
transitions between your UI states and pages. It just makes navigating
the web feel more seamless, just as it should be. And I think it's
just amazing to see.

title: Google I/O 2025 – o2wkYzu5u50

and making it easier for you
to use this API right out of the box and express custom
transitions between your UI states and pages. It just makes navigating
the web feel more seamless, just as it should be. And I think it's
just amazing to see. And we've seen many
improvements, such as, make your life as a
developer easier-- Cascade layers, CSS, Nest,
enlightened dark mode, scroll-driven animations. They're all making building UI
significantly easier, and also, more attractive. But today, I do want to talk
about some new UI primitives that I think-- that's going to make it possible
to deliver drastically better experiences for your users. These new UI primitives
will make it easier to build common UI components,
things like carousels and tooltips, and
dropdowns, the core features of almost any
single site on the web. They'll enable you to get rid
of third party dependencies, eliminate kilobytes
of JavaScript and, hopefully, improve
rendering performance, as well, as we move work
off the main thread. Now, carousels are
ubiquitous on the web. They get used millions
of times a day. So what happens if we worked out
the fundamental primitives that enable carousels and
just built them into CSS, and enable you to ship these
experiences with just CSS? Well, the good thing is that
the core functionality, features like scroll snap, they're
already on the platform. So all we really have to do is
sprinkle in some new primitives, like scroll button
and scroll marker, and now, you have a carousel. It really can be that simple. We think they're
going to also enable different types of
experiences-- tabs, and cards, and different types
of UI, all again, with the same small bit of CSS. For example, you could
build video sliders with cool parallax effects. You can build
horizontal app switches similar to what you would
find on iOS and Android when a user switches
between apps. Or you could even
do something fun, like a vertical scroll
wheel of choices. We're only just
scratching the surface

title: Google I/O 2025 – o2wkYzu5u50
text:
You can build
horizontal app switches similar to what you would
find on iOS and Android when a user switches
between apps. Or you could even
do something fun, like a vertical scroll
wheel of choices. We're only just
scratching the surface of what's possible with just
these simple additions to CSS. And I think they open up a
huge amount of possibilities, and I'm really excited
to see the progress. Obviously, another
common use case is one you saw a little
bit earlier in the demo is select boxes. Now, I remember using
select boxes in ie4. You could set a breakpoint in
Visual Studio-- not VS Code, in Visual Studio
into the JavaScript and breakpoint on your code. And if you scrolled, the scroll
bar would just stay stuck. You couldn't do anything. And that's because they
were system level widgets. And for nearly 25
years, we've seen next to no progress in this
core platform primitive. So developers have
gotten around this by re-implementing it in
JavaScript, naturally. And so we know that you
want the ability to style select boxes and bring them
into your branding of your site. And you've told us that you
want to include HTML directly in the options and provide
functionality that we've just not had on the platform before. Well, stylable select is an
accessible, standardized, and CSS stylable select element. It just gives you that ability. By using appearance-based
select as the new CSS property, it puts the select element into
a new configurable stylable state, and every part
of that base select can now be swapped out and
personalized, and even animated. And Yoon is going to talk a
lot more about this tomorrow. So the select element can also
include rich HTML content. So, for example, in the
country dropdown menu, you can include a flag
next to your country. Or when you're browsing
through a list of people, you can now include an
avatar next to their name. So with all of these
features that I've launched in the past year and
the new ones on the horizon,
speaker: unknown

title: Google I/O 2025 – o2wkYzu5u50
text:
you can include a flag
next to your country. Or when you're browsing
through a list of people, you can now include an
avatar next to their name. So with all of these
features that I've launched in the past year and
the new ones on the horizon, I really do feel like the web is
getting way more tactile and way more expressive. And Temu, which is an
e-commerce company, has been setting the
bar when it comes to applying these new primitives
to their full potential, and they've seen some
really strong gains. After launching their
integration with the multi-page application View
Transitions API-- it's a bit of a mouthful to say
all that-- they saw user session duration increased by
10%, and user page views increased by 15%. And they've also been internally
testing a slew of new APIs, including carousels, popover,
anchor positioning, and also, custom select. And they've observed
improved page performance, and a reduction to about
10% to 15% on CPU load. I just think these new
UI features are awesome, and the web is actually becoming
more stylish and responsive every single day. But there are still massive
user experience issues when it comes to common tasks. We know that filling
out forms and keeping track of your password are two
major frustrations for anyone who uses the web. So we've made some
steps to streamline these complex interactions
for your users. Autofill is one of the first
ones, is built into Chrome. And it allows the browser
to automatically fill in the website's form
fields with the person saved information from
across all the devices. You can now measure the
impact of browser autofill on your forms, and get insights
into how users interact with your forms, and so you can
make better forms from that. It gives you the data that
you need to make improvements. And we've also had
some fantastic results from partners implementing
Autofill on their sites. Our friends at Shopify know that
when customers are ready to buy, even the slightest delay, or
extra steps can stop a sale.
speaker: unknown

title: Google I/O 2025 – o2wkYzu5u50
text:
And we've also had
some fantastic results from partners implementing
Autofill on their sites. Our friends at Shopify know that
when customers are ready to buy, even the slightest delay, or
extra steps can stop a sale. And after implementing
Autofill across their platform, Shopify found that guest
checkouts using Autofill had a 40% higher checkout
completion rate than guest checkouts without Autofill. So I think Autofill wasn't
just good for their customer experience, it was great
for their business, too. And after forms, passwords
are the next big area that we've worked to improve. We're already seeing
a shift from a world-- or to a world without passwords,
and I think that's for the best because passwords come with
innate risks and challenges from people using weak
passwords, reuse passwords, or even just they're
susceptible to phishing attacks. But passwords are so
ubiquitous that a transition to a passwordless
ecosystem across the web, it's just not going
to happen overnight. So in the short term, we've
improved the Google Password Manager to help users manage
their password more easily. So I just want to give
you a little sneak peek at the automated
password change feature now that's in Chrome, which
automatically changes passwords that are stored in
Google Password Manager. So when Chrome detects that a
password has been compromised, a user will get a
suggestion to change it. And then, with
permission, Chrome will automatically
update the password on the site behind the scenes. So we're going to start with
a subset of websites in 2025, and we'll expand this to
more websites in the future. But there are just a few
small amount of changes that you can make that help
make this automated password change work more
reliably, and you'll just be able to get
adopted straight away. And also, they'll benefit
every single password manager. This is, obviously, again,
where Autofill comes in. So by using autocomplete
current password, it's been around for years, you
can help the Password Manager
speaker: unknown

title: Google I/O 2025 – o2wkYzu5u50
text:
And also, they'll benefit
every single password manager. This is, obviously, again,
where Autofill comes in. So by using autocomplete
current password, it's been around for years, you
can help the Password Manager identify the right field to
use with the saved password. And when you use
autocomplete new password for the new password
field, you can find-- you can help the
Password Manager to suggest strong passwords to
replace the old one. Now, like I said,
these annotations, they're just a web standard,
but they are an effective way to optimize your
website to work better with any password manager,
including the automated change. This works on sign
in pages, sign up pages, any browser that
supports, well, any browser and any browser with
a password manager, not just Google Password
Manager inside Chrome. But the final part of this
transition from passwords to a passwordless ecosystem, it
requires fundamentally different technologies. And passkeys and FedCM are
two of the technologies at the center of this area. We only got time to be able
to talk about passkeys today. But over the years,
we've made a number of improvements to
passkeys and how passkeys works inside the browser. So passkeys on Google
Password Manager are now synchronized and
saved across all platforms, including Windows, Mac, Linux,
Chrome OS, iOS, and Android. And websites can
now automatically request your Password Manager to
create a passkey on your behalf, as long as you already
have a password saved. And we're introducing a new API,
which will also let your users-- will also let you ask the user-- ask the user's Password
Manager to create this passkey automatically. And this will work as
long as the passkey is saved to your site, and was
also recently used to sign in. And then we'll notify
the user when the passkey has been created successfully. Now, we would
recommend that you call this API immediately
after a successful sign in with a password. And it's available in Chrome 136
across all desktop platforms,

And then we'll notify
the user when the passkey has been created successfully. Now, we would
recommend that you call this API immediately
after a successful sign in with a password. And it's available in Chrome 136
across all desktop platforms, and will be coming
to Android soon. And when the user
signs back in, you can just trigger the autofill
drop down to automatically, for any credentials that
are available on the device, including passkeys. And to make this
work, again, you just use the correct autofill
values for passwords, and also include the
passkeys autofill attribute. And then simply set the
auto focus attribute on the input field, and then
Chrome will just do the rest. So these are just a few of the
user experience improvements that we've made to the core of
the platform over the last year to make the web more consistent
and more seamless to use. And there's a lot

Federation with FedCM, the digital credentials API,
and even things like device bound cookies. So by making the web easier
and also more secure, we believe that people will
